---
title: "Sonic Time Travelers: Can a Song's Identity Place It in Its Era?"
subtitle: "JSC370 Midterm Project - University of Toronto, 2026"
author: "Anh Dang Phuong"
date: "March 7, 2026"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Table of Contents"
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    theme: cosmo
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Core
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re
import os

import requests
import time

# Spotify
import spotipy
from spotipy.oauth2 import SpotifyClientCredentials, SpotifyOAuth

# Visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px

# NLP / Embeddings
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# Modeling
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder, StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix
import xgboost as xgb

# Misc
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Plot style
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="muted")
```

---

## I. Introduction

The project is motivated by a cultural observation: just as fashion cycles repeat themselves, music appears to as well. Artists like The Weeknd or Dua Lipa are frequently noted for their retro sonic aesthetics despite being comtemporary acts. This sparks curiosity on whether this phenomenon is statistically measurable and whether it correlates with commercial succes.

### Research Question

> *"Can a song's sonic and lyrical identity reliably place it in its era, and where does the model fail? Are modern songs that 'sound old' actually distinguishable from the originals?"*

### Hypotheses

We have some predictions regarding the outcome of our study:

**Primary Hypothesis:** Modern songs mimicking 80s/90s sonics will be misclassified by the Audio Classification Model as being more "vintage" while being correctly identified by the Lyric model as "modern" due to contemporary and themes in their lyrics.

**Secondary Hypothesis:** Missclassified modern songs, which are the "neo-retro" hits, will have a significantly higher Spotify Popularity Score than correctly classified modern songs. This supports a nostalgia sells theory, which is a strategy in marketing/creative that promotes products with characteristics that could evoke emotional feelings from customers so that they will make the purchase decision more easily.

### Novelty

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  - Contrast with existing Kaggle-based Spotify projects
  - Emphasize the multi-source fusion (Spotify + Genius + MusicBrainz)
  - Emphasize API-driven reproducibility vs. static snapshots
  - The two-modality comparison angle has not been done extensively
-->

We will use data from Spotify, MusicBrainz, and Genius to TODO

---

## II. Methods

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE — brief paragraph introducing the methods section] -->
### Data Sources

In order to answer all aspects of the research question, we collect and combine data from different sources:

1. Spotify Web API

- Documentation:

- Purpose: audio features and track metadata 

- What we collect: track ID, track name, artist name, album ID, release date, popularity score, and some audio features per song such as danceability, energy, loudness, key, mode.

- Method: Search API queries by decade and genre keyword. For example, we query 80s rock tracks by calling `rock year:1980-1989`. Then pull the results by batches of 10. To respect Spotify's API rate limits (~5.5k records/day), we split the data collection process into 3-4 days to account for code bugs or unexpected failures. 

2. Genius API + Web Scraping

- Documentation:

- Purpose: Song lyrics for lyrical embedding 

- What we collect: Raw lyrics for each track, scraped from Genius song pages after searching by artist name and track name via the Genius search API

- Method: First, we query the Genius search API to find the lyrics URL. Then, we will scrape the lyrics page using BeautifulSoup. After that, the scraped lyrics are cleaned by removing section headers such as [Verse 1], non-ASCII characters, and excess whitespace using regex. Cleaning will help further steps easier.

3. MusicBraiz API

- Documentation:

- Purpose: Cultural enrichment and enhancing knowledge about the artists.

- What we collect: artis country (mb_country), area of origin (mb_area), city of origin (mb_begin_area), match confidence score (mb_score) community tags, and genre tags.

- Method: We search by artist name, then fetch the full artist record with tags. We have to put on rate limit to respect MusicBrainz policy. The artist names come from previously fetched spotify dataset, so to safe time, this collection process will run after every daily spotify collection, then append to the "master file". Match confidence score is retained to filter low-quality matches.


### Data Acquisition

#### Step 1: Seed Artists via Decade Playlists

Data collection begins with a playlist-seeding strategy using Spotify's curated decade playlists as entry points. For each decade from the 1980s to the 2020s, we identified four playlists covering distinct genre branches to ensure the resulting artist pool is not dominated by a single style. Rock, Pop, and Hip Hop are sampled consistently across all five decades given their sustained cultural presence, while decade-specific genres such as Dance (1980s), RnB (1990s), Country (2000s), EDM (2010s), and Latin (2020s) are included to capture the unique sonic flavors of each era.

| Decade | Seed Playlists | Genre |
|--------|----------------|-------|
| 1980s  | All Out 80s, 80s Rock Anthems, 80s Dance Hits, 80s Hip Hop Classics | Rock, Pop, Hip Hop, Dance |
| 1990s  | All Out 90s, 90s Rock Anthems, 90s Hip Hop Classics, 90s R&B | Rock, Pop, Hip Hop, RnB |
| 2000s  | All Out 00s, 2000s Rock, 2000s Hip Hop, 2000s Pop | Rock, Pop, Hip Hop, Country |
| 2010s  | All Out 10s, 2010s Rock, 2010s Hip Hop, 2010s Pop | Rock, Pop, Hip Hop, EDM |
| 2020s  | Today's Top Hits, 2020s Hip Hop, 2020s Pop, Hot Country | Rodck, Pop, Hip Hop, Latin |



```{python}
#| label: spotify-auth
#| echo: true
```

```{python}
import requests
import base64
import os

CLIENT_ID     = os.environ.get("SPOTIFY_CLIENT_ID")
CLIENT_SECRET = os.environ.get("SPOTIFY_CLIENT_SECRET")

# Get access token via Client Credentials (no OAuth needed)
auth_str = f"{CLIENT_ID}:{CLIENT_SECRET}"
auth_b64 = base64.b64encode(auth_str.encode()).decode()

token_res = requests.post(
    "https://accounts.spotify.com/api/token",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Basic {auth_b64}"},
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"}
)

ACCESS_TOKEN = token_res.json()["access_token"]
HEADERS = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {ACCESS_TOKEN}"}
print("Token OK:", ACCESS_TOKEN[:20], "...")
```

```{python}
import time

decade_queries = {
    "1980s": ["rock year:1980-1989", "pop year:1980-1989", "hip hop year:1980-1989", "dance year:1980-1989"],
    "1990s": ["rock year:1990-1999", "pop year:1990-1999", "hip hop year:1990-1999", "rnb year:1990-1999"],
    "2000s": ["rock year:2000-2009", "pop year:2000-2009", "hip hop year:2000-2009", "country year:2000-2009"],
    "2010s": ["rock year:2010-2019", "pop year:2010-2019", "hip hop year:2010-2019", "edm year:2010-2019"],
    "2020s": ["rock year:2020-2026", "pop year:2020-2026", "hip hop year:2020-2026", "latin year:2020-2026"],
}

all_tracks = []
```

```{python}
import time

class RateLimiter:
    """Simple token bucket — max 10 requests per 30 seconds to stay safe"""
    def __init__(self, max_calls=10, period=30):
        self.max_calls = max_calls
        self.period = period
        self.calls = []

    def wait(self):
        now = time.time()
        # drop calls outside the rolling window
        self.calls = [c for c in self.calls if now - c < self.period]
        if len(self.calls) >= self.max_calls:
            sleep_for = self.period - (now - self.calls[0]) + 0.5
            print(f"  [RateLimiter] sleeping {sleep_for:.1f}s...")
            time.sleep(sleep_for)
        self.calls.append(time.time())

limiter = RateLimiter(max_calls=10, period=30)  # conservative: 10 req/30s
```

```{python}
def spotify_get(url, params):
    """Returns response object, or None if rate limited."""
    while True:
        limiter.wait()
        res = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, params=params)

        if res.status_code == 200:
            return res

        if res.status_code == 429:
            retry_after = int(res.headers.get("Retry-After", 10))
            print(f"HMMM {retry_after}")
            print(f"  [429] Rate limited — stopping.")
            return None  # signal to outer loop to stop

        if res.status_code == 401:
            token_res = requests.post(
                "https://accounts.spotify.com/api/token",
                headers={"Authorization": f"Basic {auth_b64}"},
                data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"}
            )
            ACCESS_TOKEN = token_res.json()["access_token"]
            HEADERS["Authorization"] = f"Bearer {ACCESS_TOKEN}"
            continue

        return res


def save_and_exit(all_tracks, filename="data/raw/spotify_tracks.parquet"):
    df = pd.DataFrame(all_tracks).drop_duplicates(subset="track_id")
    df.to_parquet(filename, index=False)
    print(f"\nSaved {len(df)} tracks to {filename}")
    print(df.groupby("seed_decade")["track_id"].count())
```

```{python}
RATE_LIMITED = False
all_tracks = []

for decade, queries in decade_queries.items():
    if RATE_LIMITED: break
    for q in queries:
        if RATE_LIMITED: break
        for offset in range(0, 1000, 10):
            if RATE_LIMITED: break
            try:
                res = spotify_get(
                    "https://api.spotify.com/v1/search",
                    {"q": q, "type": "track", "limit": 10, "offset": offset}
                )

                if res is None:  # rate limited
                    RATE_LIMITED = True
                    break

                if res.status_code != 200 or not res.content:
                    print(f"  Skipping {q} offset {offset}: {res.status_code}")
                    break

                items = res.json()["tracks"]["items"]
                if not items:
                    break

                for t in items:
                    if not t or not t.get("id"):
                        continue
                    all_tracks.append({
                        "track_id":    t.get("id"),
                        "track_name":  t.get("name"),
                        "artist_id":   t["artists"][0]["id"] if t.get("artists") else None,
                        "artist_name": t["artists"][0]["name"] if t.get("artists") else None,
                        "album_id":    t["album"].get("id") if t.get("album") else None,
                        "release_date":t["album"].get("release_date") if t.get("album") else None,
                        "popularity":  t.get("popularity", None),
                        "seed_decade": decade,
                        "seed_query":  q,
                    })

                time.sleep(0.5)

            except Exception as e:
                print(f"  Exception: {e}")
                break

        print(f"Done: {decade} | {q} — running total: {len(all_tracks)}")

# Always saves no matter what
save_and_exit(all_tracks)
```

```{python}
# Load all parquet files
files = [
    "data/raw/seed_tracks.parquet",
    "data/raw/seed_tracks_partial.parquet",
    "data/raw/seed_tracks_2_partial.parquet",
    "data/raw/seed_tracks_3_partial.parquet",
    "data/raw/seed_tracks_4_partial.parquet",
]

dfs = []
for f in files:
    if os.path.exists(f):
        df = pd.read_parquet(f)
        print(f"  {f}: {len(df)} tracks")
        dfs.append(df)
    else:
        print(f"  MISSING: {f}")

combined = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True).drop_duplicates(subset="track_id")
print(f"\nTotal after deduplication: {len(combined)}")
print(combined.groupby("seed_decade")["track_id"].count())

# Save both formats
os.makedirs("data/raw", exist_ok=True)
combined.to_parquet("data/raw/spotify_tracks.parquet", index=False)
combined.to_csv("data/raw/spotify_tracks.csv", index=False)
print("\nSaved spotify_tracks.parquet and spotify_tracks.csv!")
```

#### Step 2: MusicBrainz Enrichment

To supplement Spotify's metadata with cultural context, we query the MusicBrainz API for each unique artist in our dataset. MusicBrainz is an open-source community-maintained music encyclopedia that provides structured data not available through Spotify, including the artist's country of origin, the city or region they are associated with, and community-contributed genre and style tags.

For each artist, we perform a name-based search to retrieve the MusicBrainz artist ID, then make a detailed record fetch with tags and genres included. A match confidence score is returned with each search result and retained for downstream filtering. MusicBrainz enforces a strict rate limit of one request per second, which is respected using a fixed 1.1-second sleep between calls. 

The resulting enrichment covers artist origin country, broader area, city of origin, and pipe-separated tag and genre strings.

```{python}
#| label: musicbrainz
#| echo: true

# Query MusicBrainz for a subset of artists
# Get: area (country of origin), tags, genre tags
# Merge on artist_name
# Save to data/raw/musicbrainz_raw.parquet

MB_HEADERS = {
    # MusicBrainz requires a descriptive User-Agent or they block you
    "User-Agent": "JSC370-MidtermProject/1.0 (anh.dangphuong@mail.utoronto.ca)"
}

def get_artist_mb(artist_name):
    """Query MusicBrainz for artist metadata: country of origin and tags."""
    try:
        # Step 1: search for artist by name
        search_res = requests.get(
            "https://musicbrainz.org/ws/2/artist",
            headers=MB_HEADERS,
            params={
                "query": f'artist:"{artist_name}"',
                "limit": 1,
                "fmt": "json"
            }
        )
        time.sleep(1.1)  # MusicBrainz rate limit: 1 req/sec

        if search_res.status_code != 200 or not search_res.json().get("artists"):
            return None

        artist = search_res.json()["artists"][0]
        mb_id  = artist.get("id")

        # Step 2: fetch full artist record to get tags
        detail_res = requests.get(
            f"https://musicbrainz.org/ws/2/artist/{mb_id}",
            headers=MB_HEADERS,
            params={"inc": "tags+genres", "fmt": "json"}
        )
        time.sleep(1.1)

        if detail_res.status_code != 200:
            return None

        detail = detail_res.json()
        tags   = [t["name"] for t in detail.get("tags", [])]
        genres = [g["name"] for g in detail.get("genres", [])]

        return {
            "artist_name":    artist_name,
            "mb_id":          mb_id,
            "mb_country":     artist.get("country", None),         # e.g. "US", "GB"
            "mb_area":        artist.get("area", {}).get("name"),  # e.g. "United States"
            "mb_begin_area":  artist.get("begin-area", {}).get("name"),  # city of origin
            "mb_tags":        "|".join(tags),
            "mb_genres":      "|".join(genres),
            "mb_score":       artist.get("score"),  # match confidence 0-100
        }

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"  Error on {artist_name}: {e}")
        return None
```

```{python}
# Load unique artists from seed data
# (run after seed_tracks.parquet exists)
seed_df     = pd.read_parquet("data/raw/spotify_tracks.parquet")
artists     = seed_df[["artist_id", "artist_name"]].drop_duplicates(subset="artist_id")

artists_sample = artists.sample(len(artists), random_state=42)
print(f"Querying MusicBrainz for {len(artists_sample)} artists...")

mb_results = []
for i, row in enumerate(artists_sample.itertuples()):
    result = get_artist_mb(row.artist_name)
    if result:
        result["artist_id"] = row.artist_id
        mb_results.append(result)
    if i % 50 == 0:
        print(f"  {i}/{len(artists_sample)} — {len(mb_results)} matched so far")

mb_df = pd.DataFrame(mb_results)
print(f"\nMusicBrainz matched: {len(mb_df)} / {len(artists_sample)} artists")
print(mb_df["mb_country"].value_counts().head(10))

os.makedirs("data/raw", exist_ok=True)
mb_df.to_parquet("data/raw/musicbrainz_full.parquet", index=False)
print("Saved to data/raw/musicbrainz_full.parquet")
```

```{python}
# Load all parquet files
files = [
    "data/raw/musicbrainz_raw.parquet",
    "data/raw/musicbrainz_raw_partial.parquet",
    "data/raw/musicbrainz_raw_2_partial.parquet",
    "data/raw/musicbrainz_raw_3_partial.parquet",
    "data/raw/musicbrainz_raw_4_partial.parquet",
]

dfs = []
for f in files:
    if os.path.exists(f):
        df = pd.read_parquet(f)
        print(f"  {f}: {len(df)} artists")
        dfs.append(df)
    else:
        print(f"  MISSING (skipping): {f}")

combined = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True).drop_duplicates(subset="artist_id")
print(f"\nTotal after deduplication: {len(combined)}")
print(f"Countries represented: {combined['mb_country'].nunique()}")
print(combined["mb_country"].value_counts().head(10))

os.makedirs("data/raw", exist_ok=True)
combined.to_parquet("data/raw/musicbrainz.parquet", index=False)
combined.to_csv("data/raw/musicbrainz.csv", index=False)
print("\nSaved musicbrainz.parquet and musicbrainz.csv!")
```

#### Step 3: Lyrics via Genius

Lyrics are retrieved for each track in our Spotify dataset. For each track, we first query the Genius API using the cleaned artist name and track name to obtain the lyrics page URL. Then we scrpae the page content using BeautifulSoup. Track names are pre-cleaned before querying by stripping parenthetical annotations such as "(Remastered)" or "(feat. Artist)" which tend to confuse the search results.

Songs returning fewer than 20 words after cleaning are treated as failed scrapes. A checkpoint is saved every 500 tracks so the pipeline is fully resumable in the event of interruption or API failure. 

```{python}
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

GENIUS_TOKEN = os.environ.get("GENIUS_ACCESS_TOKEN")
GENIUS_HEADERS = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {GENIUS_TOKEN}"}

def search_genius(artist_name, track_name):
  try:
    res = requests.get(
      "https://api.genius.com/search",
      headers=GENIUS_HEADERS,
      params={"q":f"{artist_name} {track_name}"},
      timeout=10
    )
    if res.status_code != 200:
      return None
    hits = res.json()["response"]["hits"]
    if not hits:
      return None
    #take first result
    return hits[0]["result"]["url"]

  except Exception:
    return None

  
def scrape_lyrics(url):
    try:
        # 1. Use a real User-Agent to avoid being blocked
        headers = {
            "User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/119.0.0.0 Safari/537.36"
        }
        res = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=15)
        if res.status_code != 200:
            return None

        soup = BeautifulSoup(res.text, "html.parser") # lxml can be finicky with broken HTML

        # 2. Look for the newer lyrics containers
        # Genius uses divs that start with 'Lyrics__Container'
        lyrics_divs = soup.find_all("div", class_=re.compile("^Lyrics__Container"))
        
        if not lyrics_divs:
            # Fallback for older layout
            lyrics_divs = soup.find_all("div", attrs={"data-lyrics-container": "true"})

        if not lyrics_divs:
            return None 

        lyrics_parts = []
        for div in lyrics_divs:
            # Handle <br> tags properly before getting text
            for br in div.find_all("br"):
                br.replace_with("\n")
            lyrics_parts.append(div.get_text(separator="\n"))

        raw_lyrics = "\n".join(lyrics_parts)
        
        # 3. Clean headers and non-ASCII (Your existing logic is good here)
        cleaned = re.sub(r"\[.*?\]", "", raw_lyrics)
        cleaned = re.sub(r"[^\x00-\x7F]+", " ", cleaned)
        cleaned = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", cleaned).strip()

        return cleaned if len(cleaned.split()) > 20 else None

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Scrape error: {e}")
        return None


def clean_track_name(name):
    # Remove text in parentheses like (Remastered), (Live), (feat. Artist)
    name = re.sub(r'\s*\([^)]*\)', '', name)
    # Remove text after dashes like - Remastered, - 2011 Mix
    name = re.sub(r'\s*-\s*.*(?:Remaster|Mix|Live|Edit|Version|Radio).*', '', name, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return name.strip()
```

```{python}
print(f"Token: {GENIUS_TOKEN[:20] if GENIUS_TOKEN else 'NOT FOUND - IS NONE'}")
```

```{python}
tracks_df = pd.read_parquet("data/raw/spotify_tracks.parquet")
total = len(tracks_df)
print(f"Total tracks to fetch lyrics for: {total}")

# Resume support — skip already fetched tracks
lyrics_file = "data/raw/lyrics_raw.parquet"
if os.path.exists(lyrics_file):
    existing = pd.read_parquet(lyrics_file)
    fetched_ids = set(existing["track_id"].tolist())
    print(f"Resuming — already fetched: {len(fetched_ids)}, remaining: {total - len(fetched_ids)}")
else:
    existing = pd.DataFrame()
    fetched_ids = set()

lyrics_results = []
failed = 0
success = 0

for i, row in enumerate(tracks_df.itertuples()):
    if row.track_id in fetched_ids:
        continue
    
    cleaned_track = clean_track_name(row.track_name)
    url = search_genius(row.artist_name, cleaned_track)
    if url is None:
        failed += 1
        print(f"  [{i+1}/{total}] ✗ NOT FOUND  — {row.artist_name} | {row.track_name} (total failed: {failed})")
        continue

    lyrics = scrape_lyrics(url)
    has_lyrics = lyrics is not None
    if has_lyrics:
        success += 1

    lyrics_results.append({
        "track_id":    row.track_id,
        "track_name":  row.track_name,
        "artist_name": row.artist_name,
        "seed_decade": row.seed_decade,
        "genius_url":  url,
        "lyrics":      lyrics,
        "has_lyrics":  has_lyrics,
    })

    status  = "✓" if has_lyrics else "✗ NO LYRICS"
    fetched = len(lyrics_results) + len(fetched_ids)
    print(f"  [{i+1}/{total}] {status} — {row.artist_name} | {row.track_name} ({row.seed_decade}) | match rate: {100*success/len(lyrics_results):.1f}%")

    # Save checkpoint every 500 tracks
    if len(lyrics_results) % 500 == 0:
        checkpoint = pd.concat(
            [existing, pd.DataFrame(lyrics_results)],
            ignore_index=True
        ).drop_duplicates(subset="track_id")
        checkpoint.to_parquet(lyrics_file, index=False)
        print(f"\n  ── Checkpoint saved — {len(checkpoint)}/{total} total | success: {success} | failed: {failed} ──\n")

    time.sleep(0.5)

# Final save
final = pd.concat(
    [existing, pd.DataFrame(lyrics_results)],
    ignore_index=True
).drop_duplicates(subset="track_id")

final.to_parquet(lyrics_file, index=False)
final.to_csv("data/raw/lyrics_raw.csv", index=False)

matched = final["has_lyrics"].sum()
print(f"\nDone — {len(final)} tracks processed")
print(f"Lyrics found:   {matched} ({100*matched/len(final):.1f}%)")
print(f"Lyrics missing: {len(final) - matched}")
print(f"Match rate by decade:")
print(final.groupby("seed_decade")["has_lyrics"].mean().round(2))
```


```{python}
#| label: genius-lyrics
#| echo: true
mb_df.head()
```

#### Step 4: Lyrical Embeddings

To represent lyrics as numerical features suitable for machine learning, we use the `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` model from `sentence transformers` library to generate dense vector embeddings of each song's full lyric text. This model maps variable-length text to a fixed vector that captures semantic meaning, making it possible to compare songs lyrically in a continuous embedding space regardless of exactly vocabulary differences. Embeddings will be cached to file after generation to save computation time later on.

```{python}
#| label: embeddings
#| echo: true

# load cleaned lyrics
lyrics_df = pd.read_parquet("data/raw/lyrics_raw.parquet")
lyrics_df = lyrics_df[lyrics_df["has_lyrics"] == True].reset_index(drop=True)

# load model
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

lyrics_list = lyrics_df["lyrics"].tolist()
embeddings = model.encode(lyrics_list, show_progress_bar=True, batch_size=64)
print(f"Embedding shape: {embeddings.shape}")  # verify that it is (N,384)

emb_df = pd.DataFrame(embeddings, columns=[f"emb_{i}" for i in range(384)])
emb_df.insert(0, "track_id", lyrics_df["track_id"].values)

os.makedirs("data/processed", exist_ok=True)
emb_df.to_parquet("data/processed/embeddings.parquet", index=False)
emb_df.to_csv("data/processed/embeddings.csv", index=False)
print(f"Saved embeddings for {len(emb_df)} songs")
```

#### Step 5: Cleaning and Labeling

With all raw data sources collected, we perform cleaning, decade labeling, and cross-source alignment in a single consolidated step. By cleaning after all fetching is complete, we avoid discarding tracks that may have failed one pipeline but could still contribute to the analysis.

First, we clean the Spotify tracks. Release years are extracted and parsed, records outside the 1980 and 2026 range are removed, and tracks identified as remasters, live versions, or remixes via regex are filtered out. Deduplication retains only the earliest known release per artist and track name pair to preserve the song's true era of origin. Decade labels are then assigned by binning release years into 5 categories. A stratified cap of 2000 songs per decade is applied to correct for the overrepresentation of the 2010s. 

The audio features, MusicBrainz, and lyrical embeddings are merged against the cleaned Spotify track list using track id and artist id. Tracks that failed MusicBrainz matching are retained in the audio and lyrics datasets with null rather than dropped, since country of origin is a supplementary feature than required. Tracks that failed Genius scraping are excluded only from the lyrics and master datasets but remain in the audio-only. 

```{python}
# load combined spotify tracks
tracks_df = pd.read_parquet("data/raw/spotify_tracks.parquet")
print(f"Raw tracks loaded: {len(tracks_df)}")

# 1. extract year from release_date
tracks_df["release_year"] = pd.to_datetime(tracks_df["release_date"], format="mixed", errors="coerce").dt.year

# drop rows where year couldn't be parsed 
tracks_df = tracks_df.dropna(subset=["release_year"])
tracks_df["release_year"] = tracks_df["release_year"].astype(int)
print(f"After dropping year: {len(tracks_df)}")

# 2. filter to 1980 - 2026 only
tracks_df = tracks_df[
  (tracks_df["release_year"] >= 1980) &
  (tracks_df["release_year"] <= 2026)
]
print(f"After filtering: {len(tracks_df)}")

# 3. remove remasters, live versions, remixes
REMASTER_PATTERN = r"remaster|live|remix|acoustic|anniversary|deluxe|version|edit"
mask = tracks_df["track_name"].str.contains(REMASTER_PATTERN, case=False, regex=True, na=False)
tracks_df = tracks_df[~mask]
print(f"After removing remaster: {len(tracks_df)}")

# 4. deduplication 
# if same song appears on multiple albums/reissues, we keep the earliest release. that will be our true original era
tracks_df = (tracks_df.sort_values("release_year").drop_duplicates(subset=["track_name", "artist_name"], keep="first"))
print(f"After deduplication: {len(tracks_df)}")

# 5. bin into decades
def bin_decade(year):
  if 1980 <= year <= 1989: return "1980s"
  if 1990 <= year <= 1999: return "1990s"
  if 2000 <= year <= 2009: return "2000s"
  if 2010 <= year <= 2019: return "2010s"
  if 2020 <= year <= 2026: return "2020s"
  return None

tracks_df["decade"] = tracks_df["release_year"].apply(bin_decade)
tracks_df = tracks_df.dropna(subset=["decade"])
print(f"\nDecade distribution (before cap):")
print(tracks_df["decade"].value_counts().sort_index())
```

```{python}
# 6. stratify cap at 2000 per decade
tracks_clean = (
    tracks_df
    .groupby("decade", group_keys=False)[tracks_df.columns]
    .apply(lambda x: x.sample(min(len(x), 2000), random_state=42))
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)
print(f"\nDecade distribution (after cap):")
print(tracks_clean["decade"].value_counts().sort_index())
print(f"\nFinal clean dataset: {len(tracks_clean)} tracks")
```

```{python}
tracks_clean
```

```{python}
# 7. merge MusicBrainz
mb_df = pd.read_parquet("data/raw/musicbrainz.parquet")
tracks_clean = tracks_clean.merge(
    mb_df[["artist_id", "mb_country", "mb_area", "mb_begin_area", "mb_tags", "mb_genres", "mb_score"]],
    on="artist_id",
    how="left"   # keep all tracks, null mb fields for unmatched artists
)
mb_matched = tracks_clean["mb_country"].notna().sum()
print(f"\nMusicBrainz matched: {mb_matched}/{len(tracks_clean)} tracks ({100*mb_matched/len(tracks_clean):.1f}%)")


# 8. merge lyrics
lyrics_df = pd.read_parquet("data/raw/lyrics_raw.parquet")
lyrics_df = lyrics_df[lyrics_df["has_lyrics"] == True][["track_id", "lyrics", "genius_url"]]
tracks_clean = tracks_clean.merge(lyrics_df, on="track_id", how="left")
lyrics_matched = tracks_clean["lyrics"].notna().sum()
print(f"Lyrics matched:      {lyrics_matched}/{len(tracks_clean)} tracks ({100*lyrics_matched/len(tracks_clean):.1f}%)")


# 9. merge embeddings
emb_df = pd.read_parquet("data/processed/embeddings.parquet")
tracks_clean = tracks_clean.merge(emb_df, on="track_id", how="left")
emb_matched = tracks_clean["emb_0"].notna().sum()
print(f"Embeddings matched:  {emb_matched}/{len(tracks_clean)} tracks ({100*emb_matched/len(tracks_clean):.1f}%)")

```

```{python}
#save
os.makedirs("data/processed", exist_ok=True)

# audio dataset: all cleaned tracks regardless of lyrics
tracks_clean.to_parquet("data/processed/tracks_clean.parquet", index=False)
tracks_clean.to_csv("data/processed/tracks_clean.csv", index=False)

# master dataset: only tracks with both audio + embeddings
master = tracks_clean[tracks_clean["emb_0"].notna()].reset_index(drop=True)
master.to_parquet("data/processed/master.parquet", index=False)

print(f"\nAudio dataset:  {len(tracks_clean)} tracks to data/processed/tracks_clean.parquet")
print(f"Master dataset: {len(master)} tracks to data/processed/master.parquet")
```

### Final Dataset Summary

After ingestion and preparation, we have 2 datasets. The audio dataset contains 9,854 tracks with complete Spotify audio features and decade labels, covering all five decades from the 1980s to the 2020s with a maximum of 2,000 tracks per decade. The master dataset contains 9,176 tracks that additionally have lyrical embeddings available, representing the intersection of the audio dataset with successfully scraped and embedded lyrics. 

As mentioned earlier, we want to extensively study both lyrics and audio characteristics. This is why we have 2 dataset. The master dataset is used for all analyses requiring both modalities, while the full audio dataset is used for the audio-only classifier to maximize training data.

| Dataset | N Songs | Features | Used For |
|---------|---------|----------|----------|
| Audio   | 9854 | 11 Spotify audio features | Audio decade classifier |
| Master  | 9176  | Audio + lyrics combined | Combined classifier + analysis |

Raw tracks loaded: 15147
After dropping year: 15147
After filtering: 15147
After removing remaster: 13721
After deduplication: 12983

Decade distribution (before cap):
decade
1980s    2542
1990s    3137
2000s    3064
2010s    1854
2020s    2386

Decade distribution (after cap):
decade
1980s    2000
1990s    2000
2000s    2000
2010s    1854
2020s    2000


Before cap, the observations are not so extreme. However, to ensure fairness, it is still fine to do capping. The lowest is 2010s with 1854 records.

Embedding coverage (ratio between the master dataset which has lyrics embeddings vs. audio dataset) is 93.1%.

```{python}
#| label: load-master
#| echo: true
audio_df = pd.read_parquet("data/processed/tracks_clean.parquet")
master_df = pd.read_parquet("data/processed/master.parquet")

print(f"Audio dataset:  {len(audio_df)} tracks")
print(f"Master dataset: {len(master_df)} tracks")
print(f"Embedding coverage: {len(master_df)/len(audio_df)*100:.1f}%")
```

### Analytical Tools

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Briefly describe:
  - Python libraries used (spotipy, sentence-transformers, scikit-learn, XGBoost, etc.)
  - Modeling approach overview (preview of three classifiers)
  - Statistical methods used in EDA (summary stats, density plots, PCA, etc.)
-->

Data collection is handled through direct HTTP calls via the requests library to the Spotify Search API, Genius API, and MusicBrainz API, with beautifulsoup used for lyrics page scraping.  Data is managed with pandas and serialized using pyarrow. Lyrical embeddings are generated with the sentence-transformers library using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model. Exploratory analysis uses matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly for visualization, and scikit-learn for preprocessing and dimensionality reduction. 
---

## III. Preliminary Results

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE — brief paragraph setting up what follows] -->

### 3.1 Summary Statistics

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Summarize key statistics in prose:
  - Final N per decade after cleaning
  - Distribution of popularity scores
  - Lyrics match rate
  Do NOT print raw dataframe output.
-->

```{python}
#| label: summary-stats
#| echo: true

master_df = pd.read_parquet("data/processed/master.parquet")
emb_cols = [c for c in master_df.columns if c.startswith("emb_")]

# counts per decade 
decade_counts = master_df["decade"].value_counts().sort_index().reset_index()
decade_counts.columns = ["Decade", "N Tracks"]

# # popularity stats per decade
# pop_stats = (
#     master_df
#     .groupby("decade")["popularity"]
#     .agg(["mean", "median", "std"])
#     .round(2)
#     .reset_index()
# )
# pop_stats.columns = ["Decade", "Mean Popularity", "Median Popularity", "Std"]

# lyrics length stats 
master_df["lyric_word_count"] = master_df["lyrics"].dropna().apply(
    lambda x: len(str(x).split())
)
lyric_stats = (
    master_df
    .dropna(subset=["lyric_word_count"])
    .groupby("decade")["lyric_word_count"]
    .agg(["mean", "median"])
    .round(1)
    .reset_index()
)
lyric_stats.columns = ["Decade", "Mean Word Count", "Median Word Count"]

# musicBrainz coverage 
mb_coverage = (
    master_df
    .groupby("decade")["mb_country"]
    .apply(lambda x: f"{x.notna().sum()} ({100*x.notna().mean():.0f}%)")
    .reset_index()
)
mb_coverage.columns = ["Decade", "MusicBrainz Coverage"]

# merge all into one summary table 
summary = (
    decade_counts
    .merge(lyric_stats, on="Decade")
    .merge(mb_coverage, on="Decade")
)

summary.style\
    .set_caption("Table 1: Summary statistics of the master dataset by decade")\
    .format(precision=2)\
    .hide(axis="index")
```

```{python}
print(master_df.groupby("decade")["popularity"].apply(lambda x: f"{x.notna().sum()}/{len(x)} ({100*x.notna().mean():.0f}%)"))
```



### 3.2 Lyric word count distribution by decade

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Describe what you see in the ridge plots below.
  Highlight: loudness war in 2000s, acousticness decline, energy shifts.
-->

```{python}
#| label: wordcount-by-decade
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Lyric word count distribution by decade"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

decade_order = ["1980s", "1990s", "2000s", "2010s", "2020s"]
palette = sns.color_palette("muted", n_colors=5)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.violinplot(
    data=master_df.dropna(subset=["lyric_word_count"]),
    x="decade", y="lyric_word_count",
    order=decade_order,
    palette=palette,
    inner="quartile",
    linewidth=0.8,
    ax=ax
)
ax.set_xlabel("Decade")
ax.set_ylabel("Word Count")
ax.set_title("Lyric Word Count Distribution by Decade")
ax.set_ylim(0, 2000)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### 3.3 PCA of Audio Feature Space

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Describe the PCA plot. Do 80s and 2020s overlap?
  This is your "scientific proof" that a classifier is needed.
-->

```{python}
#| label: tfidf-words
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Top TF-IDF terms per decade"

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
import numpy as np

decade_order = ["1980s", "1990s", "2000s", "2010s", "2020s"]

# one big document per decade
decade_docs = {
    d: " ".join(master_df[master_df["decade"] == d]["lyrics"].dropna().tolist())
    for d in decade_order
}

vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=5000,
    stop_words="english",
    min_df=2,
    ngram_range=(1, 2)   # unigrams + bigrams to catch phrases
)
tfidf_matrix = vectorizer.fit_transform(list(decade_docs.values()))
feature_names = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(22, 6))

for i, (decade, ax) in enumerate(zip(decade_order, axes)):
    scores = tfidf_matrix[i].toarray().flatten()
    top_idx = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:15]
    top_words = [feature_names[j] for j in top_idx]
    top_scores = [scores[j] for j in top_idx]

    ax.barh(top_words[::-1], top_scores[::-1], color=palette[i])
    ax.set_title(decade, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("TF-IDF Score", fontsize=9)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Top Lyrical Terms by Decade (TF-IDF)", fontsize=14, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
Without stop words, 

```{python}
#| label: tfidf-words
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Top TF-IDF terms per decade"

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer, ENGLISH_STOP_WORDS
import numpy as np

decade_order = ["1980s", "1990s", "2000s", "2010s", "2020s"]

# Custom lyrics-specific stopwords on top of sklearn's English list
LYRICS_STOPWORDS = set(ENGLISH_STOP_WORDS).union({
    # filler words
    "oh", "yeah", "ooh", "ah", "uh", "na", "la", "hey", "ha",
    "woah", "whoa", "mmm", "hm", "gonna", "wanna", "gotta",
    "ain", "ain't", "don", "don't", "didn", "didn't", "won",
    "won't", "can't", "couldn", "couldn't", "isn", "isn't",
    # generic lyric words that appear in every decade equally
    "know", "like", "just", "got", "get", "let", "come",
    "go", "say", "said", "want", "need", "love", "baby",
    "time", "way", "day", "night", "life", "heart", "feel",
    "make", "take", "give", "look", "away", "back", "right",
    "ll", "ve", "re", "em", "cause", "til", "bout",
    # chorus repetition artifacts
    "chorus", "verse", "bridge", "hook", "repeat",
    "x2", "x3", "x4",
})

decade_docs = {
    d: " ".join(master_df[master_df["decade"] == d]["lyrics"].dropna().tolist())
    for d in decade_order
}

vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=5000,
    stop_words=list(LYRICS_STOPWORDS),
    min_df=2,
    ngram_range=(1, 2)
)
tfidf_matrix = vectorizer.fit_transform(list(decade_docs.values()))
feature_names = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(22, 6))

for i, (decade, ax) in enumerate(zip(decade_order, axes)):
    scores = tfidf_matrix[i].toarray().flatten()
    top_idx = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:15]
    top_words = [feature_names[j] for j in top_idx]
    top_scores = [scores[j] for j in top_idx]

    ax.barh(top_words[::-1], top_scores[::-1], color=palette[i])
    ax.set_title(decade, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("TF-IDF Score", fontsize=9)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Top Lyrical Terms by Decade (TF-IDF)", fontsize=14, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


```{python}
#| label: country-by-decade
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Top artist countries of origin by decade"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(22, 5))

for i, (decade, ax) in enumerate(zip(decade_order, axes)):
    subset = master_df[master_df["decade"] == decade]
    top_countries = (
        subset["mb_country"]
        .dropna()
        .value_counts()
        .head(8)
    )
    ax.barh(top_countries.index[::-1], top_countries.values[::-1], color=palette[i])
    ax.set_title(decade, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("N Artists", fontsize=9)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Top Artist Countries of Origin by Decade", fontsize=14, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: wordcount-over-time
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Mean lyric word count over time"

yearly = (
    master_df
    .dropna(subset=["lyric_word_count", "release_year"])
    .groupby("release_year")["lyric_word_count"]
    .agg(["mean", "median", "count"])
    .reset_index()
)

# only keep years with at least 10 songs for stability
yearly = yearly[yearly["count"] >= 10]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

ax.plot(yearly["release_year"], yearly["mean"],
        color="#4C9BE8", linewidth=2, label="Mean")
ax.plot(yearly["release_year"], yearly["median"],
        color="#E8774C", linewidth=2, linestyle="--", label="Median")
ax.fill_between(yearly["release_year"], yearly["mean"], yearly["median"],
                alpha=0.15, color="#4C9BE8")

# shade decade bands
for start, end, label in [(1980,1990,"1980s"),(1990,2000,"1990s"),
                           (2000,2010,"2000s"),(2010,2020,"2010s"),(2020,2027,"2020s")]:
    ax.axvspan(start, end, alpha=0.05,
               color=palette[["1980s","1990s","2000s","2010s","2020s"].index(label)])
    ax.text((start+end)/2, ax.get_ylim()[0], label,
            ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=8, color="gray")

ax.set_xlabel("Release Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Word Count")
ax.set_title("Mean Lyric Word Count Over Time")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: umap-embeddings
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "UMAP projection of lyrical embeddings colored by decade"

import umap
import plotly.express as px

emb_cols = [c for c in master_df.columns if c.startswith("emb_")]
X = master_df[emb_cols].values
labels = master_df["decade"].values

reducer = umap.UMAP(n_components=2, random_state=42, n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1)
embedding_2d = reducer.fit_transform(X)

umap_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "UMAP1":       embedding_2d[:, 0],
    "UMAP2":       embedding_2d[:, 1],
    "decade":      master_df["decade"].values,
    "track_name":  master_df["track_name"].values,
    "artist_name": master_df["artist_name"].values,
})

fig = px.scatter(
    umap_df, x="UMAP1", y="UMAP2",
    color="decade",
    hover_data=["track_name", "artist_name"],
    opacity=0.5,
    title="UMAP Projection of Lyrical Embeddings by Decade",
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Pastel,
    category_orders={"decade": decade_order}
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=3))
fig.update_layout(legend_title="Decade")
fig.show()
```

### 3.4 Lyrical Era Markers: TF-IDF Word Analysis

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Describe the top words per decade.
  Highlight interesting era markers: "cassette" vs "DM", "fax" vs "TikTok"
-->

```{python}
#| label: tfidf-words
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Top TF-IDF terms per decade"

# [YOUR CODE HERE]
# TfidfVectorizer per decade
# Bar charts or word clouds showing top terms per decade
```

### 3.5 Preliminary Classification Results

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Describe the confusion matrix results.
  Which decades are most confused with each other?
  Where does the audio model predict the wrong era for modern songs?
-->

```{python}
#| label: prelim-confusion
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Confusion matrix: Audio Model decade predictions"

# [YOUR CODE HERE]
# Train a quick logistic regression baseline on audio features
# Plot confusion matrix as heatmap
```

### 3.6 Nostalgia Sells? Popularity by Misclassification

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Describe the density plot.
  Are misclassified modern songs more popular than correctly classified ones?
  Preliminary evidence for/against the secondary hypothesis.
-->

```{python}
#| label: popularity-density
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Popularity distribution: correctly vs. misclassified modern songs"

# [YOUR CODE HERE]
# Subset to 2010s + 2020s songs
# Label: correctly classified vs. predicted as 80s/90s
# Density plot of popularity scores for both groups
```

---

## IV. Summary & Final Project Plan

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Summarize what you have found so far:
  - Do audio features carry decade signal? (yes/no, what features?)
  - Do lyrical clusters align with audio clusters? (preliminary answer)
  - Any early evidence for the nostalgia hypothesis?
-->

### Key Findings So Far

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE] -->

### Final Project Plan

#### II. Data Modeling

##### 1. Splitting the Dataset

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  - 70/15/15 stratified split
  - Artist-level leakage prevention: all songs by one artist go to same split
  - Why this matters: prevents model from memorizing artist style
-->

##### 2. Training the Models

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Three parallel classifiers:
  - Audio Model: logistic regression baseline + XGBoost on 11 audio features
  - Lyric Model: PCA(50) on 384-dim embeddings + XGBoost
  - Combined Model: concatenated feature vector
  5-fold cross-validated grid search for hyperparameters
-->

##### 3. Validation

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  - Macro F1 as primary metric (class imbalance)
  - Early stopping for XGBoost
  - Per-class F1 to identify hardest decades
-->

#### III. Performance Evaluation

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  - Confusion matrices for all three models
  - SHAP values for feature importance
  - "Sonic Time Traveler" score: |audio_predicted_decade - lyric_predicted_decade|
    Example: Audio says 1980s, Lyrics say 2020s → score = 40 years
  - Nostalgia sells test: t-test + Cohen's d on popularity scores
  - ARI between audio and lyric cluster assignments
  - UMAP projections colored by true decade, predicted decade, Time Traveler score
-->

#### IV. Interaction Component

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  Three interactive components on the final Quarto website:
  1. "What Era Does This Song Belong To?" — user inputs Spotify URL, 
     gets predicted decade + confidence bar chart
  2. "Sonic Time Traveler" Explorer — interactive UMAP scatter plot,
     hover reveals song name, true era, audio-predicted era, lyric-predicted era
  3. Nostalgia Leaderboard — sortable table of top 50 highest Time Traveler score songs
-->

---

## References

<!-- [YOUR TEXT HERE]
  - Spotify Web API documentation: https://developer.spotify.com/documentation/web-api
  - MusicBrainz API documentation: https://musicbrainz.org/doc/MusicBrainz_API
  - Genius API
  - sentence-transformers / all-MiniLM-L6-v2
  - Any academic papers on music information retrieval or cultural era analysis
-->
